rationale: The citing paper uses Huber, Payne, and Puto (2014) in a focused conceptual comparison, not just a background list. It cites that work's finding of inconsistent attraction effects under conditions like highly desirable withdrawn options and strong pre-existing preferences, then uses this to argue why the jilting effect is theoretically distinct from phantom-option/attraction paradigms. This citation therefore meaningfully informs the paper's positioning and contribution boundaries, even though the cited method is not directly reused.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on Huber et al. (2014) to characterize limits in attraction-effect findings and then positions its jilting framework as a different mechanism and research domain. The citation functions as a substantive conceptual contrast used to define what the new theory is and is not.
